perturbation.original_text: During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the Recipient agrees to treat confidentially information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the Recipient or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the User agrees to treat confidentially information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the User or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.explanation: Here, even though the NDA defines 'Recipient' as the receiving party, the text is modified to state that 'User' (which now is supposed to mean the receiving party) agrees to treat information confidentially and prepares documents. But, the Confidential Information section keeps the 'Recipient' term, creating confusion: Is the User now the Recipient, or are the obligations separate? Also, the part about the notes being used by the Disclosing party's review or interest is on the Recipient, the question then arises; is it on the User or the Recipient?
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the evaluation of the Proposed Transaction, the User agrees to treat confidentially information that is furnished by either the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Recipient or the Recipient’s subsidiaries or its or their respective directors, officers, partners, employees, agents, representatives, including, without limitation, financial advisors, counsel, accountants, experts, and consultants (collectively, “Representatives”) in connection with the Proposed Transaction, together with notes, analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by the User or by its Representatives to the extent they contain or otherwise reflect such information or the Disclosing Party’s review of, or interest in, the Recipient (“Confidential Information”).
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Recipient' as the receiving party. The changed text replaces 'Recipient' with 'User' when referring to the party that agrees to treat information confidentially and prepares documents. However, the contradicted text still uses both 'User' and 'Recipient'. This creates a direct contradiction because it's unclear whether 'User' and 'Recipient' refer to the same entity or if they have separate obligations, as well as introducing uncertainty regarding which party the obligation of the disclosing party's review falls on. Therefore, following the Misaligned Terminology definition, this is a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The public disclosure of the Proposed Transaction could have a material adverse effect on IDT’s and PLX’s businesses if, for any reason, a definitive agreement with respect to such transaction is not consummated. Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Representatives not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no Confidential Information will be provided to such persons. The term “person” shall be broadly interpreted March 31, 2012 to include, without limitation, any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The public disclosure of the Proposed Transaction could have a material adverse effect on IDT’s and PLX’s businesses if, for any reason, a definitive agreement with respect to such transaction is not consummated. Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Agents not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no Confidential Information will be provided to such persons. The term “person” shall be broadly interpreted March 31, 2012 to include, without limitation, any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Representatives' to 'Agents' creates a conflict. Section 1 defines 'Representatives' broadly. Now, Section 3 introduces 'Agents'. Are 'Agents' a subset of 'Representatives', or something entirely separate? This is especially problematic because the section refers to 'its Representatives', but introduces 'Agents' when refering to who the party will direct
perturbation.contradicted_text: The public disclosure of the Proposed Transaction could have a material adverse effect on IDT’s and PLX’s businesses if, for any reason, a definitive agreement with respect to such transaction is not consummated. Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Agents not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no Confidential Information will be provided to such persons. The term “person” shall be broadly interpreted March 31, 2012 to include, without limitation, any corporation, governmental agency or body, partnership or individual.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Representatives' with 'Agents'. The explanation states that 'Representatives' is defined elsewhere, and introducing 'Agents' creates ambiguity. The text requires directing 'Agents' but also mentions 'Representatives', leading to confusion about who is being directed, and creating a conflict by using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Private Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 5, 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Private Information'. This is problematic because the agreement hinges on the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The rest of the agreement refers only to 'Confidential Information', leaving 'Private Information' undefined and its protection ambiguous. Also the last part of the sentence makes reference to 'Confidential Information hereunder' creates a contradition with the first part of the phrase.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Private Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party. No license under any trade secrets, copyrights, or other rights is granted by this Agreement or any disclosure of Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Private Information' in the Changed Text creates a contradiction. The Contradicted Text refers to 'Confidential Information hereunder', which is no longer consistent with the changed term 'Private Information' in the first part of the Changed Text. Since the contract refers only to 'Confidential Information' and now the term 'Private Information' is introduced, that implies that there is an in-text contradition and aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' which occurs when the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement. Return and/or destruction of Confidential Information and copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof and documents, memoranda, notes, and other writings to the extent that they contain Confidential Information shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an authorized officer of the Recipient supervising such destruction upon the Disclosing Party’s written request.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the User shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement. Return and/or destruction of Confidential Information and copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof and documents, memoranda, notes, and other writings to the extent that they contain Confidential Information shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an authorized officer of the User supervising such destruction upon the Disclosing Party’s written request.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the Recipient by the User, creates a contradiction where even thought the NDA defines the Receiving party as the User, the document refers to the Recipient. This is problematics since the Recipient also retains the Confidential Information copies after Termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the User shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement. Return and/or destruction of Confidential Information and copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof and documents, memoranda, notes, and other writings to the extent that they contain Confidential Information shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an authorized officer of the User supervising such destruction upon the Disclosing Party’s written request.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'Recipient' consistently. The changed text replaces 'Recipient' with 'User' in one instance, but not throughout the clause. The contradicted text uses a mix of 'User' and 'Recipient' within the same clause. This creates a contradiction because the clause now refers to two potentially different parties, 'User' and 'Recipient', regarding obligations and rights related to confidential information, leading to ambiguity about who is responsible for destruction/return and retention of the information, and who is supervising such destruction, creating a strong in-text contradiction per the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants including but not limited to (i) advertising on the internet or in a newspaper or periodical of general circulation, (ii) an employee of an executive search firm acting on behalf of the hiring company where the hiring company did not instruct or encourage such solicitation, (iii) a general hiring program conducted by the hiring party in the ordinary course of business (such as a job fair or the like), or (iv) a response by the hiring party if the employee approaches the hiring party on an unsolicited basis.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Proprietary Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants including but not limited to (i) advertising on the internet or in a newspaper or periodical of general circulation, (ii) an employee of an executive search firm acting on behalf of the hiring company where the hiring company did not instruct or encourage such solicitation, (iii) a general hiring program conducted by the hiring party in the ordinary course of business (such as a job fair or the like), or (iv) a response by the hiring party if the employee approaches the hiring party on an unsolicited basis.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' in Section 9's consideration clause creates uncertainty. The entire agreement is predicated on protecting 'Confidential Information'. If the non-solicitation agreement is based on 'Proprietary Information', which is never defined, it's unclear what information is being protected by the non-solicitation clause. Also, the introduction of 'Proprietary Information' as consideration clashes with the rest of the NDA, which focuses solely on 'Confidential Information'. This creates ambiguity about whether the non-solicitation agreement even has valid consideration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the Proprietary Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements. The prohibition contained in this paragraph does not extend to general solicitations of employment by a party not specifically directed towards the other party’s employees or consultants including but not limited to (i) advertising on the internet or in a newspaper or periodical of general circulation, (ii) an employee of an executive search firm acting on behalf of the hiring company where the hiring company did not instruct or encourage such solicitation, (iii) a general hiring program conducted by the hiring party in the ordinary course of business (such as a job fair or the like), or (iv) a response by the hiring party if the employee approaches the hiring party on an unsolicited basis.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Information' in the consideration clause of a non-solicitation agreement. The contradicted text also shows 'Proprietary Information'. The explanation states that this creates uncertainty because the entire agreement is predicated on protecting 'Confidential Information', which is defined elsewhere, while 'Proprietary Information' is not defined. This creates ambiguity, because both texts use the same "consideration" and makes it unclear whether the non-solicitation agreement has valid consideration, thus creating an in-text contradiction based on Misaligned Terminology.
